nodes.title: Start with an empty output string
nodes.why: Strings are immutable, so we build a new one instead of editing in place.
nodes.alternative: Push characters into an array and join at the end.
nodes.tradeoff: Very readable, but repeated concatenation can be slower on huge strings.

nodes.title: Iterate from the end to the beginning
nodes.why: Reading characters in reverse order naturally produces the reversed result.
nodes.alternative: Two-pointer swap on an array copy.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple control flow, but requires careful index boundaries.

nodes.title: Append the current character each step
nodes.why: Each iteration adds one character, gradually building the final reversed string.
nodes.alternative: Prepend instead of append while looping forward.
nodes.tradeoff: Append is easy to reason about with a backward loop.

nodes.title: Split string into characters
nodes.why: `reverse()` works on arrays, so we first convert the string into a character list.
nodes.alternative: Keep everything in string form and use a manual loop.
nodes.tradeoff: Convenient API chain, but adds conversion overhead.

nodes.title: Reverse the array in place
nodes.why: This reorders elements to mirror the string from back to front.
nodes.tradeoff: Short and idiomatic, but disallowed as a primary method in some interviews.

nodes.title: Join characters back into a string
nodes.why: After reversing the array, we convert it back to the final string output.
nodes.alternative: Return the array and let caller join.
nodes.tradeoff: Cleaner function contract, at the cost of an extra conversion step.
